questions.question: Explain the three main components of a production system with a suitable diagram.
keyPoints: Production Rules (condition-action pairs) - IF-THEN rules representing knowledge
Working Memory (facts database) - stores current facts and assertions about problem state
Rule Interpreter (inference engine) - controls the recognition-action cycle

questions.question: Differentiate between forward chaining and backward chaining with an example.
keyPoints: Forward Chaining: Data-driven, works from facts to conclusions, breadth-first exploration
Backward Chaining: Goal-driven, works from goal backwards to facts, depth-first exploration
Example: Medical diagnosis - FC starts from symptoms to find disease; BC starts from suspected disease to verify with symptoms

questions.question: What is conflict resolution in production systems? Explain any two control strategies used for conflict resolution.
keyPoints: Conflict resolution: selecting which rule to fire when multiple rules are applicable
Control strategies: Lexicographic ordering - rules have priority numbers, Specificity ordering - more specific rules prioritized
Recency ordering - rules using recent facts prioritized

questions.question: Describe the recognition-action cycle (Match-Resolve-Act) in production systems.
keyPoints: Match: Compare all rules against working memory to find applicable rules
Resolve: Apply conflict resolution strategy to select one rule from applicable rules
Act: Execute the selected rule, updating working memory with new facts

questions.question: Explain the working memory component of a production system. What type of information does it store?
keyPoints: Working memory (blackboard/facts database) stores current state information
Contains: Initial facts, intermediate results, derived facts, assertions
Dynamic - changes as rules fire and new facts are added or old facts modified

questions.question: What are production rules? How are they structured? Give an example.
keyPoints: Production rules are IF-THEN condition-action pairs
Structure: IF <conditions> THEN <actions> or IF <conditions> THEN <assertions>
Example: IF fever AND cough THEN suspect_influenza

questions.question: Explain the recency ordering control strategy with an example.
keyPoints: Recency ordering prioritizes rules that use facts most recently added to working memory
Assumption: Recent facts are more relevant to current problem state
Example: If fact 'object_is_red' was added recently, rule with this condition gets higher priority

questions.question: Describe the architecture of a production system. Explain in detail the recognition-action cycle with a neat flowchart.
keyPoints: Architecture: Production Rules Database, Working Memory, Rule Interpreter (Inference Engine)
Recognition-Action Cycle: (1) Match - find all rules whose conditions are satisfied by current facts
(2) Conflict Resolution - select one rule from applicable rules using control strategy
(3) Act - execute the selected rule, add new facts to working memory
Cycle repeats until no rules applicable or goal reached
Flowchart showing Match → Resolve → Act → Update WM → Repeat

questions.question: Discuss forward chaining and backward chaining in detail. Compare their advantages, disadvantages, and applications.
keyPoints: Forward Chaining: Data-driven, starts from facts, explores forward, suitable for monitoring/prediction
Advantages: Good for open-ended problems, comprehensive exploration
Disadvantages: May generate irrelevant conclusions, inefficient
Backward Chaining: Goal-driven, starts from goal, works backwards, suitable for diagnosis/troubleshooting
Advantages: Focused on goal, efficient for specific problems
Disadvantages: May miss relevant facts if goal is wrong
Applications: FC - monitoring systems, process control; BC - diagnostic systems, query systems

questions.question: Explain the various control strategies used for conflict resolution in production systems with examples.
keyPoints: Lexicographic Ordering: Rules have priority numbers, highest priority rule selected (e.g., Rule1 > Rule2 > Rule3)
Specificity Ordering: More specific rules (more conditions) have higher priority (e.g., rule with 3 conditions > rule with 1 condition)
Recency Ordering: Rules using recently added facts have higher priority
Refraction: Rule that just fired cannot fire again until relevant facts change
Context Limiting: Only certain rules allowed in certain contexts
Combined Strategies: Multiple strategies can be combined for better resolution

questions.question: Design a simple production system for a medical diagnosis problem. Show sample production rules, working memory states, and explain the inference process using forward chaining.
keyPoints: Production Rules: IF symptom='fever' AND symptom='cough' THEN suspect='flu'
Working Memory: Initial facts like symptom(fever), symptom(cough)
Inference Process: Match rules, find applicable rules, resolve conflicts, execute rule, add new facts
Shows how suspected diagnosis is added to working memory
Can continue with more rules to confirm or rule out diagnosis
